name: Mock Test - 02
instruction: This question paper contains three sections - A, B and C. Each part is compulsory. Section - A has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20. Section - B has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20 Section - C has 10 MCQs, attempt any 8 out of 10. There is no negative marking. All questions carry equal marks.

questions.question_text: A relation R on a non – empty set A is an equivalence relation if it is
questions.question_html: A relation R on a non – empty set A is an equivalence relation if it is
questions.explanation: By definition of Equivalence Relation, a relation is said to be equivalence if it is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.

questions.question_text: Corner points of the feasible region for an LPP are (0, 2), (3, 0), (6, 0), (6, 8) and (0, 5). Let F = 4x + 6y be the objective function. Maximum of F – Minimum of F =
questions.question_html: Corner points of the feasible region for an LPP are (0, 2), (3, 0), (6, 0), (6, 8) and (0, 5). Let F = 4x + 6y be the objective function. Maximum of F – Minimum of F =
questions.explanation: Here the objective function is given by : F = 4x +6y . Corner points Z = 4x +6 y (0, 2 ) 12(Min.) (3,0) 12.(Min.) (6,0 ) 24 (6 , 8 ) 72 (0 , 5 ) 30 Maximum of F – Minimum of F = 72 – 12 = 30 .

questions.question_text: Derivative of cos(sin x) w.r.t. sin x is
questions.question_html: Derivative of cos(sin x) w.r.t. sin x is
questions.explanation: Let y = cos(sinx), z = sinx , then , {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dz}} = \frac{{\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}}}{{\frac{{dz}}{{dx}}}} = \frac{{ - \sin (\sin x)\cos x}}{{\cos x}} = - \sin (\sin x){/tex}

questions.question_text: The vertices of a a ABC are A(-2, 4), B( 2, -6) and C(5, 4). The area of a ABC is
questions.question_html: The vertices of a a ABC are A(-2, 4), B( 2, -6) and C(5, 4). The area of a ABC is
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{rrr} -2 & 4 & 1 \\ 2 & -6 & 1 \\ 5 & 4 & 1 \end{array}\right|={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{rrr} -2 & 4 & 1 \\ 4 & -10 & 0 \\ 7 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 35 sq units

questions.question_text: The minimum value of f(x) = x 4 - x 2 - 2x + 6 is
questions.question_html: The minimum value of f(x) = x4 - x2 - 2x + 6 is
questions.explanation: f(x) = x 4 - x 2 - 2x + 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 4x 3 - 2x - 2 put f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x 3 - 2x -2 = 0 (x - 1) (4x 2 + 4x + 2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 f"(x) = 12x 2 - 2 f"(1) = 10 > 0 local minima at x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(1) = 4

questions.question_text: The circumference of a circle is measured as 28 cm with an error of 0.01 cm. The percentage error in the area is
questions.question_html: The circumference of a circle is measured as 28 cm with an error of 0.01 cm. The percentage error in the area is
questions.explanation: Given that circumference is = C = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = 28 cm That means r = {tex}\frac{14}\pi{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} C = 2 {tex}\pi\Delta{/tex} r = 0.01 {tex}\Delta{/tex} r = ( {tex}\frac{0.01}{2\pi}{/tex} ) We all know that area of a circle is = A = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 {tex}\Delta{/tex} A= 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} dr So finally, {tex}\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}\right) \times 100=2 \times \frac{\frac{0.01}{2 \pi}}{\frac{14}{\pi}} \times 100{/tex} = {tex}\frac1{14} {/tex}

questions.question_text: Let X 1 and X 2 are optimal solutions of a LPP, then
questions.question_html: Let X1 and X2 are optimal solutions of a LPP, then

options.text: {tex}X=\lambda X_{1}+(1-\lambda) X_{2}, \lambda \in R{/tex} is also an optimal solution
options.html: {tex}X=\lambda X_{1}+(1-\lambda) X_{2}, \lambda \in R{/tex} is also an optimal solution

options.text: {tex}X=\lambda X_{1}+(1+\lambda) X_{2}, \lambda \in R{/tex} gives an optimal solution
options.html: {tex}X=\lambda X_{1}+(1+\lambda) X_{2}, \lambda \in R{/tex} gives an optimal solution

options.text: {tex}X=\lambda X_{1}+(1+\lambda) X_{2}, 0 \leq \lambda \leq 1{/tex} give an optimal solution
options.html: {tex}X=\lambda X_{1}+(1+\lambda) X_{2}, 0 \leq \lambda \leq 1{/tex} give an optimal solution

options.text: {tex}X=\lambda X_{1}+(1-\lambda) X_{2}, 0 \leq \lambda \leq 1{/tex} gives an optimal solution
options.html: {tex}X=\lambda X_{1}+(1-\lambda) X_{2}, 0 \leq \lambda \leq 1{/tex} gives an optimal solution

questions.correct_option_text: {tex}X=\lambda X_{1}+(1-\lambda) X_{2}, 0 \leq \lambda \leq 1{/tex} gives an optimal solution
questions.explanation: X = {tex}\lambda X_{1}+(1-\lambda) X_{2}{/tex} , 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\leq{/tex} 1 gives an optimal solution A set 'A' is convex if, for any two points, x 1 , x 1 {tex}\in{/tex} A, and {tex}\lambda \in[0,1]{/tex} imply that {tex}\lambda \mathrm{x}_{1}+(1-\lambda) x_{2} \in A{/tex} since, here X 1 and X 2 are optimal solutions Therefore, their convex combination will also be an optimal solution Thus, X = {tex}\lambda X_{1}+(1-\lambda) X_{2}{/tex} , 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\leq{/tex} 1 gives an optimal solution.

questions.question_text: Rank of a non-zero matrix is always
questions.question_html: Rank of a non-zero matrix is always
questions.explanation: Rank of a non zero matrix is always greater than or equal to 1.

questions.question_text: Let R be the feasible region for a linear programming problem,and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. If R is bounded, then the objective function Z has both a maximum and a minimum value on R and
questions.question_html: Let R be the feasible region for a linear programming problem,and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. If R is bounded, then the objective function Z has both a maximum and a minimum value on R and

options.text: each of these occurs at a corner point (vertex) of R.
options.html: each of these occurs at a corner point (vertex) of R.

options.text: each of these occurs at the midpoints of the edges of R
options.html: each of these occurs at the midpoints of the edges of R

options.text: each of these occurs at some points except corner points of R.
options.html: each of these occurs at some points except corner points of R.

options.text: each of these occurs at the centre of R.
options.html: each of these occurs at the centre of R.

questions.correct_option_text: each of these occurs at a corner point (vertex) of R.
questions.explanation: Let R be the feasible region for a linear programming problem,and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. If R is bounded, then the objective function Z has both a maximum and a minimum value on R and each of these occurs at a corner point (vertex) of R.

questions.question_text: If y = cot -1 {tex}\left(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\right){/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If y = cot-1 {tex}\left(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\right){/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Given that {tex}y=\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\right){/tex} Let x = {tex}\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x{/tex} and using {tex}\cot ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1} x{/tex} Hence, {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right){/tex} Using {tex}\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)=\frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}{/tex} , we obtain {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}+\tan ^{-1} x{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}y=\frac{a x+b}{x^{2}+c}{/tex} , then (2xy 1 + y) y 3 =
questions.question_html: If {tex}y=\frac{a x+b}{x^{2}+c}{/tex}, then (2xy1 + y) y3 =
questions.explanation: {tex}y=\frac{a x+b}{x^{2}+c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x 2 + c) = ax + b Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x we get y 1 (x 2 + c) + 2xy = a Again differentiating w.r.t to x we get y 2 (x 2 +c) + 2xy 1 + 2y + 2xy 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 (x 2 + c) = -(4xy 1 + 2y) ...(i) Again differentiating w.r.t to x we get y 3 (x 2 + c) + 2xy 2 + 4xy 2 + 4y 1 + 2y 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 3 (x 2 + c) = -(6xy 2 + 6y 1 ) ...(ii) {tex}\frac{y_{2}}{y_{3}}=\frac{2 x y_{1}+y}{3 x y_{2}+3 y_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 3 (2xy 1 + y) = 3y 2 (xy 2 + y 1 )

questions.question_text: If y = {tex}\sqrt{x \sin x}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = ?
questions.question_html: If y = {tex}\sqrt{x \sin x}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = ?
questions.explanation: Given that y = {tex}\sqrt{x \sin x}{/tex} Squaring both sides, we obtain y 2 = xsinx Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain 2y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = xcos x + sinx or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x \cos x+\sin x}{2 y}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x \cos x+\sin x}{2 \sqrt{x \sin x}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If there is an error of a% in measuring the edge of a cube, then percentage error in its surface is:
questions.question_html: If there is an error of a% in measuring the edge of a cube, then percentage error in its surface is:
questions.explanation: Given that % Error in measuring the edge of a cube [ {tex}(\frac{\Delta L}L) \times{/tex} 100] is = a (if L is edge of the cube) We have to find out ( {tex}(\frac{\Delta A}A) \times{/tex} 100 = ? (If let the surface of the cube is A) By the formula of approximation of derivation we get, {tex}\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}\right) \times 100=2 \times\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}\right) \times 100{/tex} = 2a

questions.question_text: The value of {tex}\tan \left\{\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}-\sin ^{-1} \frac{4}{\sqrt{17}}\right\} {/tex} is
questions.question_html: The value of {tex}\tan \left\{\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}-\sin ^{-1} \frac{4}{\sqrt{17}}\right\} {/tex} is
questions.explanation: To find the value of {tex}\tan \left\{\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}-\sin ^{-1} \frac{4}{\sqrt{17}}\right\}{/tex} Put, {tex}\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}=u{/tex} and {tex}\sin ^{-1} \frac{4}{\sqrt{17}}=v{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}=\cos u{/tex} and {tex}\frac{4}{\sqrt{17}}=\sin v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan u = 7 and tan z = 4 Using properties of trigonometry. {tex}\tan \left\{\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}-\sin ^{-1} \frac{4}{\sqrt{17}}\right\}{/tex} = tan (y - z) tan (y - z) {tex}=\frac{\tan y-\tan z}{1+\tan y \tan z}=\frac{7-4}{1+28}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{29}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If from Lagrange’s mean value theorem, we have {tex}{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{b})-\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})}{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{a}}{/tex} , then
questions.question_html: If from Lagrange’s mean value theorem, we have {tex}{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{b})-\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})}{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{a}}{/tex}, then
questions.explanation: {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{b})-\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})}{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{a}}{/tex} In the Lagrange’s Mean Value Theorem, c {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b) such that {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}{/tex} f is continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (a, b), then there exists a real number x {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b) Therefore, in the case of x 1 , {tex}\mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{b})-\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})}{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{a}}{/tex} , then x 1 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b) {tex}\therefore{/tex} a < x 1 < b. This is the required solution.

questions.question_text: The point on the curve y 2 = 4x which is nearest to the point (2,1) is
questions.question_html: The point on the curve y2 = 4x which is nearest to the point (2,1) is
questions.explanation: y 2 = 4x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = {tex}\sqrt{(x-2)^{2}+(y-1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d 2 = (x - 2) 2 + (y - 1) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d 2 = {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y - 1) 2 Let u = {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right)^{2}{/tex} + (y - 1) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d y}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right) \frac{y}{2}{/tex} + 2(y - 1) To find minima {tex}\frac{d u}{d y}{/tex} = 0 2 {tex}\left(\frac{y^{2}}{4}-2\right) \frac{y}{2}{/tex} + 2(y - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 {tex}\left(x=\frac{y^{2}}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} u}{d y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 y^{2}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} u}{d y^{2}}\right)_{(1,2)}{/tex} = 3 > 0 Hence, nearest point is (1, 2).

questions.question_text: Let A = {1, 2, 3} and R = {(1, 2), (2, 3), (1, 3)} be a relation on A. Then, R is
questions.question_html: Let A = {1, 2, 3} and R = {(1, 2), (2, 3), (1, 3)} be a relation on A. Then, R is
questions.explanation: R = {(1, 2), (2, 3), (1, 3)} satisfying only the property of transitive relation. i.e Its transitive i.e (a,b) {tex} \in {/tex} R and (b,c) ∈ R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (a,c) {tex} \in {/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a,b,c {tex} \in {/tex} A

questions.question_text: The distance moved by a particle travelling in a straight line in t seconds is given by s = 45t + 11t 2 - t 3 . The time taken by the particle to come to rest is
questions.question_html: The distance moved by a particle travelling in a straight line in t seconds is given by s = 45t + 11t2 - t3. The time taken by the particle to come to rest is
questions.explanation: {tex}s=45 t+11 t^{2}-t^{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d s}{d t}=45+22 t-3 t^{2}{/tex} Given that particle moves in a straight line. {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d s}{d t}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 t^{2}-22 t-45=0{/tex} by solving the above quadratic equation we get {tex} t=9 \text { or } t \neq \frac{-5}{3}{/tex} (time cannot be negative) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 9 sec

questions.question_text: Maximize Z = 100x + 120y , subject to constraints 2x + 3y ≤ 30, 3x + y ≤ 17, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0.
questions.question_html: Maximize Z = 100x + 120y , subject to constraints 2x + 3y ≤ 30, 3x + y ≤ 17, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0.
questions.explanation: We have , Maximize Z = 100x + 120y , subject to constraints 2x + 3y ≤ 30, 3x + y ≤ 17, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0. Corner points Z = 100x +120 y P( 0 , 0 ) 0 Q(3 , 8) 1260………………….(Max.) R( 0, 10 ) 1200 S(17/3 , 0 ) 1700/3 Hence the maximum value is 1260

questions.question_text: The equation of tangent to the curve y (1 + x 2 ) = 2 - x, where it crosses x-axis is:
questions.question_html: The equation of tangent to the curve y (1 + x2) = 2 - x, where it crosses x-axis is:
questions.explanation: We have, equation of the curve y(1 + x 2 ) = 2 - x ...(i) It is given that the curve crosses x-axis Putting y = 0 in equation (i), we get {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0(1 + x 2 ) = 2 - x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 So the curves passes through the point (2, 0) Now differentiating equation (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\therefore y \times(0+2 x)+\left(1+x^{2}\right) \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0 - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1-2 x y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(2,0)}=\frac{-1-2 \times 0}{1+2^{2}}=-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} = slope of tangent to the curve {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of tangent of the curve passing through (2, 0) is y - 0 = {tex}-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} (x - 2) or x + 5y = 2

questions.question_text: Let {tex}f(x)=\frac{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)}{\cot 2 x}, x \neq \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} The value which should be assigned to f(x) at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} , so that it is continuous everywhere is
questions.question_html: Let {tex}f(x)=\frac{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)}{\cot 2 x}, x \neq \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} The value which should be assigned to f(x) at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, so that it is continuous everywhere is
questions.explanation: If f(x) is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} f(x)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)}{\cot 2 x}=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} If {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}-x=y{/tex} then {tex}x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and {tex}y \rightarrow 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lim _\limits{y \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\tan y}{\cot 2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-y\right)}\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{y \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\tan y}{\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-2 y\right)}\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{y \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\tan y}{\tan 2 y}\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{y \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\frac{\tan y}{y}}{\frac{\tan 2 y}{y}}\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{y \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\frac{\tan y}{y}}{\frac{2 \tan 2 y}{2 y}}\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \lim _\limits{y \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\frac{\tan y}{y}}{\frac{\tan 2 y}{2 y}}\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\lim _{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{\tan y}{y}}{\lim _{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{\tan 2 y}{2 y}}\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{1}\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The principal value of cot -1 {tex}(-\sqrt{3}){/tex} is
questions.question_html: The principal value of cot-1 {tex}(-\sqrt{3}){/tex} is
questions.explanation: Let the principle value be given by x also, let x = cot -1 {tex}(-\sqrt{3}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot x = {tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot x = -cot {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} cot {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\sqrt{3}{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot x = cot {tex}\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} - cot ( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = cot ( {tex}\pi-\theta{/tex} )) {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{5 \pi}{6}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If A and B are square matrices of the same order then (A + B)(A - B) = ?
questions.question_html: If A and B are square matrices of the same order then (A + B)(A - B) = ?
questions.explanation: Since A and B are square matrices of same order. (A + B)(A - B) = A 2 - AB + BA - B 2

questions.question_text: If {tex}y=\sqrt{\sin x+y}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If {tex}y=\sqrt{\sin x+y}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\because y=(\sin x+y)^{1/2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}(\sin x+y)^{-1 / 2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x+y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{(\sin x+y)^{1/2}} \cdot\left(\cos x+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 y}\left(\cos x+\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\left[\because(\sin x+y)^{1 / 2}=y\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(1-\frac{1}{2 y}\right)=\frac{\cos x}{2 y}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos x}{2 y} \cdot \frac{2 y}{2 y-1}=\frac{\cos x}{2 y-1}{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: The value of c in Mean value theorem for the function f (x) = x (x - 2), x {tex}\in{/tex} [1, 2] is
questions.question_html: The value of c in Mean value theorem for the function f (x) = x (x - 2), x {tex}\in{/tex} [1, 2] is
questions.explanation: We have f'(c) = {tex}\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}{/tex} Here a = 1, b = 2 Put the value of c in f'(x) = 2x -2 Hence, 2c - 2 = {tex}\frac{f(2)-f(1)}{2-1}{/tex} = 2(2 - 2) - (-1) = 2(c - 1) = 1 c = 3\2

questions.question_text: The two curves x 3 - 3xy 2 + 2 = 0 and 3x 2 y - y 3 - 2 = 0 intersect at an angle of
questions.question_html: The two curves x3 - 3xy2 + 2 = 0 and 3x2y - y3 - 2 = 0 intersect at an angle of
questions.explanation: Given equation of curves are x 3 - 3xy 2 + 2 = 0 ...(i) and 3x 2 y - y 3 - 2 = 0 ...(ii) Differentiating equation (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}3 x^{2}-3 x \cdot 2 y \frac{d y}{d x}-3 y^{2} \cdot 1+0=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}=\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} Differentiating equation (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}3 x^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}+6 x y-3 y^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}+0=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=\frac{2 x y}{y^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex} Now {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=-1{/tex} Hence, the curves intersect at right angle.

questions.question_text: The value of sin {tex}\left( {2{{\cos }^{ - 1}}\left( { - \frac{3}{5}} \right)} \right){/tex} is
questions.question_html: The value of sin{tex}\left( {2{{\cos }^{ - 1}}\left( { - \frac{3}{5}} \right)} \right){/tex} is
questions.explanation: put {tex}\;{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( { - \frac{3}{5}} \right) = \theta \implies\cos \theta \; = - \frac{3}{5}\;{/tex} therefore the given expression is {tex}\;\sin 2\theta \; = 2sin\theta cos\theta = \frac{{2.4}}{5}.\left( { - \frac{3}{5}} \right) = \left( { - \frac{{24}}{{25}}} \right).\;{/tex}

questions.question_text: If (A - 2B) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 3 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and (2A - 3B) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -2 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} then B = ?
questions.question_html: If (A - 2B) ={tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 3 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and (2A - 3B) ={tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -2 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} then B = ?
questions.explanation: {tex}(A-2 B)=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -2 \\ 3 & 0 \end{array}\right){/tex} Multiplying equation by 2 {tex}2 A-4 B=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -4 \\ 6 & 0 \end{array}\right){/tex} ...(i) {tex}2 A-3 B=\left(\begin{array}{cc} -2 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 \end{array}\right){/tex} ...(ii) (ii) - (i) {tex}B=\left(\begin{array}{cc} -2 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 \end{array}\right)-\left(\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -4 \\ 6 & 0 \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} -4 & 6 \\ -3 & -3 \end{array}\right){/tex}

questions.question_text: The corner points of the feasible region determined by the following system of linear inequalities: 2x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 10, x + 3y {tex}\le{/tex} 15, x, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 are (0, 0), (5, 0), (3, 4) and (0, 5). Let Z = px + qy, where p, q {tex}\ge{/tex} 0. Condition on p and q so that the maximum of Z occurs at both (3, 4) and (0, 5) is
questions.question_html:
The corner points of the feasible region determined by the following system of linear inequalities:
2x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 10, x + 3y {tex}\le{/tex} 15, x, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 are (0, 0), (5, 0), (3, 4) and (0, 5). Let Z = px + qy, where p, q {tex}\ge{/tex} 0. Condition on p and q so that the maximum of Z occurs at both (3, 4) and (0, 5) is
questions.explanation: The maximum value of Z is unique. It is given that the maximum value of Z occurs at two points (3,4) and (0,5) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Value of Z at (3, 4) = Value of Z at (0, 5) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p(3) + q(4) = p(0) + q(5) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3p + 4q = 5q {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = 3p

questions.question_text: If the value of a third order determinant is 11, then the value of the square of the determinant formed by the cofactors will be
questions.question_html: If the value of a third order determinant is 11, then the value of the square of the determinant formed by the cofactors will be
questions.explanation: Since the matrix formed by the cofactors is adj(A) and we know that |adj(A)|=|A| 2 for third order square matrix. So, |adj(A)| 2 = |A| 4 and |A|=11 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |adj(A)| 2 =(11) 4 =14641

questions.question_text: For a real number x, let [x] denote the greatest integer less than or equal to x and f (x) = {tex}\frac{{\tan (\pi [x - \pi ])}}{{1 + {{[x]}^2}}}{/tex} ,then
questions.question_html: For a real number x, let [x] denote the greatest integer less than or equal to x and f (x) = {tex}\frac{{\tan (\pi [x - \pi ])}}{{1 + {{[x]}^2}}}{/tex},then

options.text: f ‘(x) exists for all x but f ‘’(x) does not exist
options.html: f ‘(x) exists for all x but f ‘’(x) does not exist

options.text: continuous at all x but f ‘(x) does not exist
options.html: continuous at all x but f ‘(x) does not exist

questions.explanation: Since {tex} [x- \pi ]{/tex} is an integer for all {tex} x \in R\; \&\; \tan n\pi \; = 0\;\forall\;n\; \in I\ {/tex} .Therefore, f(x)= 0 for all x in R. So, f(x) is a constant and hence derivatives of f(x) of all order exist.

questions.question_text: The function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} x^{2} a & , \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 0 \leq x<1 \\ a & , \quad 1 \leq x<\sqrt{2} \\ \frac{2 b^{2}-4 b}{x^{2}} & , \quad \sqrt{2} \leq x<\infty \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous for {tex}0 \leq x<\infty,{/tex} then the most suitable values of a and b are
questions.question_html: The function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} x^{2} a & , \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 0 \leq x<1 \\ a & , \quad 1 \leq x<\sqrt{2} \\ \frac{2 b^{2}-4 b}{x^{2}} & , \quad \sqrt{2} \leq x<\infty \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous for {tex}0 \leq x<\infty,{/tex} then the most suitable values of a and b are
questions.explanation: f(x) is continuous at x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} f(x)=f(1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(1+h)=a{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1+h)^{2}}{a}=a{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{a}=a{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow a=\pm 1{/tex} Consider, {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \sqrt{2}} \frac{2 b^{2}-4 b}{x^{2}}=a{/tex} b 2 - 2b = {tex}\pm 1{/tex} for a = 1 using formulas for quadratic equation {tex}b^{2}-2 b-1=0 \Rightarrow b=1 \pm \sqrt{2}{/tex} for a = -1 b 2 - 2b = -1 b 2 - 2b + 1 = 0 (b - 1) 2 = 0 b = 1 a = -1, b = 1

questions.question_text: The range of {tex}f(x) = \frac{{x - 3}}{{3 - x}},x \ne 3\;{/tex} is
questions.question_html: The range of {tex}f(x) = \frac{{x - 3}}{{3 - x}},x \ne 3\;{/tex} is
questions.explanation: We have , {tex}f(x) = \frac{{x - 3}}{{3 - x}},x \ne 3{/tex} , {tex} \Rightarrow f(x) = \frac{{ - (3 - x)}}{{(3 - x)}} \Rightarrow f(x) = - 1{/tex} . Therefore , range of f(x) isR- {-1}. Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: If 3tan –1 x + cot –1 x = {tex}\pi{/tex} , then x equals
questions.question_html: If 3tan–1 x + cot–1 x = {tex}\pi{/tex}, then x equals
questions.explanation: We know that {tex}tan^{-1}x + cot^{-1}x = \frac {\pi}2{/tex} Now, {tex}3 tan^{-1}x+cot^{-1}x= \pi\\ \Rightarrow 2tan^{-1}x+tan^{-1}x+cot^{-1}x= \pi\\ \Rightarrow 2tan^{-1}x + \frac{\pi}2= \pi\\ \Rightarrow 2tan^{-1}x=\frac {\pi}2\\ \Rightarrow tan^{-1}x= \frac{\pi}4\\ \Rightarrow x=tan\frac{\pi}4\\ \Rightarrow x=1 {/tex}

questions.question_text: The optimal valuie of the objective function is attained at the points
questions.question_html: The optimal valuie of the objective function is attained at the points

options.text: given by intersection of inequations with y-axis only
options.html: given by intersection of inequations with y-axis only

options.text: given by corner points of the feasible region
options.html: given by corner points of the feasible region

options.html: given by intersection of inequations with the axes only

options.html: given by intersection of inequations with x-axis only

questions.correct_option_text: given by corner points of the feasible region
questions.explanation: It is known that the optimal value of the objective function is attained at any of the corner point. Thus, the optimal value of the objective function is attained at the points given by corner points of the feasible region.

questions.question_text: Rolle’s theorem is applicable in case of ϕ (x) = a sinx , a > 0 in
questions.question_html: Rolle’s theorem is applicable in case of ϕ (x) = asinx, a > 0 in
questions.explanation: Φ(x) = a Sin x , a > 0 Differentiating the above-mentioned function, with respect to ‘x’, we get {tex}\Phi{/tex} '(x) = (cos x a Sin x ) . log a Let Φ’(c) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log a (cos c a Sin c ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos c a Sin c = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos c = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\operatorname{cosc}=\cos \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{c}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Also, the above-mentioned function, is derivable and continuous on the interval [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ]. Thus, here Rolle’s Theorem is applicable on the above mentioned function in the interval [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ].

questions.question_text: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 0 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 4 & -3 \\ -6 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} are such that 4A + 3X = 5B then X = ?
questions.question_html: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 0 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 4 & -3 \\ -6 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} are such that 4A + 3X = 5B then X = ?
questions.explanation: 4A + 3X = 5B {tex}\Rightarrow 4\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 0 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]+3 x=5\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -3 \\ -6 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 X=5\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -3 \\ -6 & 2 \end{array}\right]-4\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 0 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 X=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 20 & -15 \\ -30 & 10 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 8 & 0 \\ -12 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 X=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 12 & -15 \\ -18 & 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Dividing by 3 {tex}\Rightarrow X=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -5 \\ -6 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

questions.question_text: Let R be the relation in the set N given by R = {(a, b) : a = b – 2, b > 6}.
questions.question_html: Let R be the relation in the set N given by R = {(a, b) : a = b – 2, b > 6}.
questions.explanation: (6, 8) {tex}\in{/tex} R as b - 2 = 8 - 2 = 6 and b>6 .

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 46 to 50: Three schools DPS, CVC and KVS decided to organize a fair for collecting money for helping the flood victims. They sold handmade fans, mats and plates from recycled material at a cost of ₹25, ₹100 and ₹50 each respectively. The numbers of articles sold are given as: School /Article DPS CVC KVS Handmade fans 40 25 35 Mats 50 40 50 Plates 20 30 40 What is the total money (in ₹) collected by the school DPS?
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 46 to 50:

Three schools DPS, CVC and KVS decided to organize a fair for collecting money for helping the flood victims. They sold handmade fans, mats and plates from recycled material at a cost of ₹25, ₹100 and ₹50 each respectively. The numbers of articles sold are given as:

School /Article DPS CVC KVS
Handmade fans 40 25 35
Mats 50 40 50
Plates 20 30 40

What is the total money (in ₹) collected by the school DPS?
